We conduct experiments on competition-level code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks with state-of-the-art LLMs, which are covered in section §3.1 and §3.2, respectively.

We also conduct experiments with community models like Llama38b, StableCode3b, and CodeLlama34b. We use the instruct variant of these models and inference on a single A100-80G under BF16 precision with vLLM.

FUNCODER surpasses HumanEval and MBPP in basic programming questions by +3.3% in Pass@1 and reduces the error rate by 18.6%. FUNCODER demonstrates a substantial improvement on competition-level problems, outperforming others by 10.4% in GPT-4 and 35.3% with GPT-3.5.

Functional consensus and self-testing are compared in terms of their ability to evaluate correctness and select the best implementation. Functional consensus is shown to be effective in evaluating correctness and selecting promising solutions in the first attempt.

Functional consensus and self-testing are compared in terms of their effectiveness in measuring ranking algorithms for selecting functions. Functional consensus achieves 94.7% upper bound performance by selecting a single function, which is close to the performance of self-testing.

Functional consensus and self-testing are compared in terms of their implementation in generating correct and reliable unit tests. It is shown that self-testing methods have limitations, leading to the adoption of functional consensus for mutual verification between solutions.

Functional consensus and the concept of dividing and conquering are evaluated in the context of an ablation study in FUNCODER. The study aims to analyze the impact of these concepts on the overall system and performance.
